tiles.title: Cost last month
tiles.description: Your last month's bill in a nutshell. Billed cost is what appears on your invoice. Effective cost accounts for amortized commitments. The difference matters for accurate showback.

tiles.title: Cost over time
tiles.description: Billed vs effective cost over time. When these diverge, it means your commitment purchases are being amortized. Understanding this gap is key to explaining invoices to finance.

tiles.title: Change over time
tiles.description: Is your bill growing or shrinking? Each bar shows the change from the previous month. Positive bars mean the bill grew. A string of positive bars means costs are consistently rising.

tiles.title: Counts
tiles.description: How many subscriptions, resources, and resource groups were active last month? Use this to sanity-check your bill - if resource count dropped but cost didn't, pricing changed.

tiles.title: Counts (last n days)
tiles.description: How big is our cloud footprint right now? These counts tell you how many subscriptions, resource groups, resources, and services had charges recently. If a count suddenly jumps, someone spun up something new.

tiles.title: Effective cost
tiles.description: This is what your organization actually paid each month after all discounts. Each bar is one billing period. If a bar is taller than the one before it, spending went up - find out why.

tiles.markdownText:
# FinOps hubs 14.0 (March 2026)
The FinOps hub dashboard provides several summaries of your cost and usage in alignment with the [FinOps Framework](https://aka.ms/finops/fx). All data formatted based on the [FinOps Open Cost and Usage Specification (FOCUS)](https://aka.ms/finops/focus).

Most of this dashboard is designed to work with effective (amortized) cost rather than billed (actual) cost. Amortization breaks down reservation and savings plan purchases and allocates costs to the resources that received the benefit to facilitate chargeback reporting. Effective costs will not match your invoice. For invoice reconciliation, refer to the **Invoicing** page.

### Understanding Amortized vs Actual Cost
| FOCUS Column | Azure Cost Management View | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **BilledCost** | Actual cost | What appears on your invoice. Commitment purchases show as lump sums. |
| **EffectiveCost** | Amortized cost | Commitment purchases are spread across benefiting resources. Best for chargeback. |

If this dashboard shows a different total than Azure Cost Management, check which cost view you are comparing: **Actual cost** = `BilledCost`, **Amortized cost** = `EffectiveCost`. The difference equals the amortized portion of commitment discount purchases (reservations, savings plans).

[Learn more](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs)     [Give feedback](https://portal.azure.com/#view/HubsExtension/InProductFeedbackBlade/extensionName/FinOpsToolkit/cesQuestion/How%20easy%20or%20hard%20is%20it%20to%20use%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/cvaQuestion/How%20valuable%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/surveyId/FTK13.0/bladeName/Hubs.Dashboard/featureName/Summary)

tiles.title: About the FinOps toolkit
tiles.markdownText:
FinOps hubs are part of the [FinOps toolkit](https://aka.ms/finops/toolkit), an open-source collection of FinOps solutions that help you manage and optimize your cost, usage, and carbon.

To contribute to the FinOps toolkit, [join us on GitHub](https://aka.ms/ftk).

tiles.title: 3-month running total trend
tiles.description: Watch your money accumulate day by day across 3 months. The shape of each curve tells you if you spend steadily or if costs spike at certain times. Steeper slopes mean faster burn.

tiles.title: Running total - This month and last
tiles.description: Are you spending faster this month than last? The two curves show cumulative daily spend. If this month's line is above last month's at the same day, you're on pace to spend more.

tiles.title: 3-month daily trend
tiles.description: Your day-by-day spending overlaid for 3 months. Look for repeating patterns (weekday vs weekend) and outlier spikes. Spikes usually mean someone deployed something expensive or a job ran long.

tiles.title: 3-month daily trend by subscription
tiles.description: Same daily view, but colored by subscription. This tells you which team or environment is responsible when you see a cost spike. The biggest colored bands are your top spenders.

tiles.title: Cost + savings running total (this month and last)
tiles.description: Are you saving more this month than last month? This overlays your cumulative cost and savings side by side. If the savings area is growing faster, your optimization efforts are working.

tiles.title: Summary
tiles.description: Your headline numbers: total cost, total savings, and how much changed vs. last month. Share these in your monthly FinOps review. Green means costs went down; red means they went up.

tiles.title: Summary
tiles.description: The same headline numbers in a table so you can compare month by month. Look at the change column - consistent increases mean you need to investigate, not just accept growth.

tiles.title: Summary
tiles.description: Month-by-month cost with change percentages. Use this to prep for budget reviews. Consistent month-over-month increases mean you need to plan for growth, not just react to it.

tiles.title: Effective cost by subscription
tiles.description: Each subscription's monthly cost for budget allocation. If you do chargeback or showback, this is the data to share with subscription owners.

tiles.title: SKUs with commitment discounts (last n days)
tiles.description: These SKUs are covered by reservations or savings plans. Look at utilization - if a commitment discount is barely used, it's wasting money instead of saving it.

tiles.title: SKUs with no discounts (last n days)
tiles.description: These SKUs are paying full on-demand prices with zero discounts. This is your biggest optimization opportunity - buy reservations or savings plans for the ones you use consistently.

tiles.description: SKUs ranked by how many resources use them. High-count SKUs are predictable demand - exactly what reservations and savings plans are designed for. Start commitment purchases here.

# Disk SKU comparison (Preview)
Compare Premium SSD v1 vs Premium SSD v2 costs to identify potential savings from migrating disk tiers. Premium SSD v2 offers lower cost per provisioned IOPS and throughput for workloads that don't need guaranteed burst performance.

[Learn more about Premium SSD v2](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/virtual-machines/disks-types#premium-ssd-v2)

tiles.title: Disk tier cost comparison (last n days)
tiles.description: Summary comparison of Premium SSD v1 vs v2 costs. Shows total spend, disk count, and average cost per disk for each tier. If v1 cost is significantly higher, migration to v2 could save money.

tiles.title: Disk tier detail by SKU (last n days)
tiles.description: Detail breakdown of disk costs by SKU description and tier. Shows each disk SKU, its tier classification (Premium SSD v1/v2), resource count, total cost, and cost per disk.

tiles.title: Ingested data by table
tiles.description: How much data is in each ADX table? This tells you which FOCUS datasets (costs, prices, recommendations, transactions) are being ingested. Missing datasets mean incomplete visibility.

tiles.title: Ingested cost data by scope
tiles.description: Which billing scopes (subscriptions/accounts) have cost data in the hub? If a scope is missing, its costs won't appear in any dashboard. Check your export pipeline.

tiles.title: Ingested scopes
tiles.description: How many billing scopes are feeding data into the hub? If this is lower than the number of subscriptions you manage, some scopes aren't exporting data yet.

tiles.title: Ingested months
tiles.description: How many months of historical data are available? More months means better forecasts and trend analysis. If this is low, your exports may have just started recently.

tiles.title: Ingested price data by scope
tiles.description: Price sheet data by scope. Without price data, the hub can't calculate list costs accurately. Scopes missing here will have incomplete cost breakdowns.

tiles.title: Ingested recommendation data by scope
tiles.description: Advisor recommendation data by scope. Without this, the hub can't surface optimization suggestions. Make sure your key subscriptions export recommendations.

tiles.title: Ingested transaction data by scope
tiles.description: Transaction/invoice data by scope. This powers the invoicing views. If scopes are missing, billing reconciliation will be incomplete for those accounts.

tiles.title: Ingested commitment discount usage data by scope
tiles.description: Commitment discount usage data by scope. Without this, reservation and savings plan utilization analysis will be incomplete. Ensure all committed scopes export this.

tiles.title: About this domain
tiles.markdownText: Data ingestion refers to the process of collecting, transforming, and organizing data from various sources into a single, easily accessible repository. With this capability, you identify data sources needed to support your FinOps practice and facilitate FinOps tasks. [Learn more](http://aka.ms/ftk/fx/ingestion)

# Azure Hybrid Benefit
Summary of the Hybrid Benefit coverage and utilization

tiles.description: Do you have Windows/SQL licenses you already own? Hybrid Benefit lets you reuse them in Azure for up to 40% savings. These numbers show how much of your estate is using it.

tiles.title: Underutilized vCPU capacity (last n days)
tiles.description: These resources have Hybrid Benefit enabled but aren't fully using the licensed vCPUs. You're paying for licenses that aren't being utilized - resize or redistribute.

tiles.title: Fully utilized vCPU capacity (last n days)
tiles.description: Well-optimized resources that are fully using their Hybrid Benefit licenses. This is what 'good' looks like - use this as a benchmark for the rest.

Invoicing and chargeback refer to the process of receiving, reconciling, and paying provider invoices, and then billing internal teams for their respective cloud costs using existing internal finance tools and processes. With this capability, you break down invoices and cross-charge portions of these invoices to the teams responsible for the charges.

[Learn more](http://aka.ms/ftk/fx/chargeback)     [Give feedback](https://portal.azure.com/#view/HubsExtension/InProductFeedbackBlade/extensionName/FinOpsToolkit/cesQuestion/How%20easy%20or%20hard%20is%20it%20to%20use%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/cvaQuestion/How%20valuable%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/surveyId/FTK13.0/bladeName/Hubs.Dashboard/featureName/Manage.Invoicing)

Budgeting is the process of monitoring and managing financial plans and limits over a specific period to control spending effectively. This capability allows you to use established estimates and forecasts to allocate funds to each team for fiscal year planning and set up alerts to ensure budget accountability. This capability plays a key role in staying within established fiscal constraints on an ongoing basis.

[Learn more](http://aka.ms/ftk/fx/budgeting)     [Give feedback](https://portal.azure.com/#view/HubsExtension/InProductFeedbackBlade/extensionName/FinOpsToolkit/cesQuestion/How%20easy%20or%20hard%20is%20it%20to%20use%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/cvaQuestion/How%20valuable%20the%20FinOps%20hubs%20Data%20Explorer%20dashboard%3F/surveyId/FTK13.0/bladeName/Hubs.Dashboard/featureName/Understand.Quantify.Budgeting.Nav)

tiles.title: Forecast (next n days)
tiles.description: Where is spending headed? This uses your historical patterns to predict future costs. If the forecast line is climbing, now is the time to act - not after the bill arrives.

tiles.title: Effective Savings Rate (last n months)
tiles.description: What percentage of your total potential cost are you avoiding through discounts? Industry target is 30-50%. Below 20% means significant untapped savings.

tiles.title: Savings breakdown by month
tiles.description: Month-by-month detail of where savings come from. Use this in your monthly FinOps review to show progress and identify which savings categories are growing or shrinking.

tiles.title: Effective Savings Rate (last n days)
tiles.description: Your recent effective savings rate. Compare to the monthly rate - if the daily rate is dropping, a reservation may have expired or workloads changed recently.

tiles.title: Ingested months
tiles.description: Hub health at a glance: data version, how many months are available, and whether ingestion is running normally. If months stopped incrementing, your pipeline may be broken.

tiles.title: Effective cost breakdown by month
tiles.description: Breaks down what you paid by charge type: usage, purchases, amortization. If 'purchase' charges are high but savings are low, your commitments may not be well-matched.

# SaaS & Marketplace Spending

This page tracks spending on Azure Marketplace products, third-party SaaS subscriptions, Databricks, Fabric, and managed applications. Use this to manage vendor relationships, identify SaaS sprawl, control shadow IT, and support procurement planning.

[Learn more about Marketplace](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/marketplace/)

tiles.title: SaaS Spend Summary
tiles.description: How much are you spending on marketplace SaaS products? These are software subscriptions billed through Azure. Check if you still need all of them - SaaS sprawl is real.

tiles.title: SaaS Spend by Month
tiles.description: Monthly SaaS spending trend. Rising bars might mean new SaaS subscriptions were added. Make sure each one has an owner and is still being actively used.

tiles.title: Top Publishers
tiles.description: Which SaaS vendors cost you the most? If a vendor's bar is large, make sure you're getting value from that product. Consider negotiating volume pricing for your top publishers.

tiles.title: Top Products
tiles.description: Individual SaaS products ranked by cost. Check if teams are using competing products for the same purpose - consolidating can save money and reduce complexity.

tiles.title: SaaS Spend by Subscription
tiles.description: Which subscriptions are buying SaaS products? This helps you track who's purchasing marketplace software and ensure it goes through proper procurement.

tiles.description: Preview KPI for Databricks-related spend detected in marketplace and service metadata.

# Spot Instance Analysis

Spot instances (also called preemptible VMs) offer significant discounts for interruptible workloads. This page tracks spot usage, savings achieved, and helps identify opportunities to expand spot coverage.

[Learn more about Spot VMs](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/virtual-machines/spot-vms)

tiles.title: Spot Summary
tiles.description: Spot instances can save up to 90% vs on-demand. These numbers show how much you're using spot today. Low spot usage in dev/test or batch workloads is a missed opportunity.

tiles.title: Pricing Category Breakdown
tiles.description: What percentage of your spend is on-demand vs. spot vs. committed? A large on-demand slice means there's room to save by moving predictable workloads to commitments or spot.

tiles.title: Spot vs On-Demand Trend
tiles.description: Is your spot adoption growing over time? The spot band should be expanding as teams move batch and fault-tolerant workloads to spot instances for savings.

tiles.title: Top Spot Resources
tiles.description: Your most expensive spot-priced resources. Spot can be evicted, so make sure these workloads can handle interruptions. If they can't, they should use a different pricing model.

# MACC (Microsoft Azure Consumption Commitment) Tracking

Track your MACC commitment progress, consumption trends, and forecast completion.

## Manual Entry Instructions

If you don't have API access to MACC data, you can manually enter your commitment details from your MACC contract document.

### What You Need From Your Contract:
| Item | Where to Find |
|------|---------------|
| **Commitment Amount** | Look for "Azure Consumption Commitment" or "Minimum Consumption Commitment" |
| **Start Date** | When your MACC term begins |
| **End Date** | When your MACC expires (typically 1-3 years) |
| **Monthly Consumption** | Azure Portal -> Cost Management + Billing -> Invoices |

### How to Enter Data:
1. Open **ManualEntry_MACC.kql** in the scripts folder
2. Update the values in Step 1 with your MACC details
3. Run the script in Azure Data Explorer
4. Use the queries below to track your progress

[Learn about MACC](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/cost-management-billing/manage/track-consumption-commitment)     [Give feedback](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs/feedback)

tiles.title: MACC Current Status (Preview)
tiles.description: How much of your Azure commitment (MACC) have you consumed vs. what's remaining? If you're behind schedule, you need to accelerate Azure usage or risk losing the commitment benefit.

tiles.title: Monthly Consumption Trend (Preview)
tiles.description: Are you consuming your MACC fast enough? Each bar is a month of consumption. If bars are shrinking, you're falling behind. If they're growing, you're on track.

tiles.title: MACC Forecast (Preview)
tiles.description: At your current consumption rate, will you meet your MACC commitment? If projected completion is after the deadline, you need a plan to increase eligible Azure consumption.

# Tag Tag Allocation
Analyze cost allocation using resource tags. Identify tagging gaps and break down cost by tag key and value.

Top [Top](?tile=e1a2b3c4-0001-4f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c)      [Give feedback](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs/feedback)

tiles.title: Tag Coverage
tiles.description: What percentage of your cost is tagged? Untagged cost can't be allocated to teams. If coverage is below 80%, you have a governance problem that makes chargeback unreliable.

tiles.title: Cost by tag key (last n months)
tiles.description: Which tag keys cover the most spending? 'Environment', 'CostCenter', 'Owner' should ideally be at the top. If important tags are missing, push for a tagging policy.

tiles.title: Cost by tag value (last n months)
tiles.description: What are the actual tag values and how much cost is behind each? 'Production' is usually the top value. If 'Unknown' or blank values are high, tagging needs improvement.

tiles.title: Tag detail (last n months)
tiles.description: Every tag key-value combination with its cost. Use this to build chargeback reports. If a team claims they don't spend much, show them their tagged costs here.

tiles.title: Monthly tag coverage trend
tiles.description: Is tagging getting better or worse over time? The 'tagged' band should be growing. If 'untagged' is growing, new resources are being deployed without proper tags.

tiles.markdownText:
# Resource Resource Drilldown
Drill into resource-level cost under each subscription. Identify the top resources driving cost.

Top [Top](?tile=e1a2b3c4-0001-4f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c)      [Give feedback](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs/feedback)

tiles.title: Subscription summary (last n months)
tiles.description: How many subscriptions you have, what they cost in total, and the average cost per subscription. If your average is high, some subscriptions may need attention.

tiles.title: Cost by subscription (last n months)
tiles.description: Subscription cost trend over time. Watch for subscriptions whose bands are growing - those teams may need help optimizing. Flat bands are well-managed.

tiles.title: Top resources by subscription (last n months)
tiles.description: Drill into any subscription to see its most expensive resources. This is your investigation table - when a subscription's costs jump, look here to find the culprit.

tiles.title: Resource cost by service (last n months)
tiles.description: Services ranked by total cost. The top bar is where you'll get the biggest return on optimization effort. If you're not already optimizing that service, start there.

tiles.title: Resource cost by resource group (last n months)
tiles.description: Resource groups ranked by cost. Share this with team leads - each group usually maps to an app or team. The top bars are the teams who should be doing cost reviews.

tiles.markdownText:
# Up Forecasting
Cost forecasting helps predict future cloud spending using historical trends and time-series decomposition. Use these projections to anticipate budget needs, identify spending trajectory changes, and align cloud costs with business planning cycles.

> **How it works**: Forecasts use ADX `series_decompose_forecast()` which applies seasonal decomposition and trend analysis on your historical cost data to project future daily costs. The **Monthly Budget** parameter (top of page) enables budget vs. projection comparisons.

[Learn FinOps Framework: Forecasting](https://www.finops.org/framework/capabilities/forecasting/)   |   [Feedback Give feedback](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs/feedback)

tiles.title: Current month projection
tiles.description: Based on what you've spent so far this month, this projects your end-of-month total. If it's over budget, you still have days left to take action and reduce spend.

tiles.title: 90-day cost forecast
tiles.description: Where will spending be in 3 months? This statistical forecast uses your historical patterns. Use it for capacity planning and to justify optimization initiatives.

tiles.title: Monthly forecast vs actual
tiles.description: How accurate were previous forecasts? If forecasts consistently underpredict, your budgets are probably too low. If they overpredict, you may be over-provisioning.

tiles.title: Projected cost by subscription
tiles.description: Each subscription's projected monthly cost. Flag any subscription projected to exceed its budget allocation and have a conversation with the owner now, not next month.

tiles.title: Monthly cost trend
tiles.description: Long-term spending trend. Look for seasonality (end-of-quarter spikes?) and growth rate. This helps you set realistic budgets and explain trends to leadership.

tiles.markdownText:
# Unit Unit Economics
Unit economics measures the cost efficiency of your cloud resources by calculating cost-per-unit metrics. These ratios help identify over-provisioned subscriptions, resource groups with low utilization, and resource types that are disproportionately expensive.

> **Metrics**: Cost/Resource = total cost / distinct resources. Cost/Subscription = total cost / active subscriptions. Cost/RG = total cost / resource groups. Lower ratios indicate better cost distribution. Rising trends may signal waste.

[Learn FinOps Framework: Unit Economics](https://www.finops.org/framework/capabilities/unit-economics/)   |   [Feedback Give feedback](https://aka.ms/finops/hubs/feedback)

tiles.title: Cost efficiency KPIs (last month)
tiles.description: How efficiently are you using cloud resources? High waste ratio means you're paying for unused capacity. Low utilization means resources are oversized. Focus on the worst KPI first.

tiles.title: Cost efficiency by subscription
tiles.description: Which subscriptions have the worst efficiency? These teams should be first in line for optimization reviews. High-cost, low-efficiency subscriptions are your biggest opportunity.

tiles.title: Monthly efficiency trend
tiles.description: Is your efficiency improving or declining over time? After optimization efforts, this trend should improve. If it's flat, your efforts aren't having measurable impact.

tiles.title: Cost efficiency by resource group
tiles.description: Which resource groups (apps/teams) are most wasteful? Share this with engineering leads - they often don't know their resources are underutilized until they see the data.

tiles.title: Unit cost by resource type
tiles.description: What's the average cost per resource for each type? If your average VM cost is rising but you haven't changed SKUs, something is wrong - investigate pricing or usage changes.

tiles.markdownText:
### Budget Budget Tracking
Set your **Monthly Budget** parameter (top of page) to enable budget vs actual comparisons. Variance thresholds: On track (<5%), At risk (5-15%), Over budget (>15%). Budget is split equally across subscriptions for per-subscription tracking.

tiles.title: Budget vs actual
tiles.description: Are you over or under budget? Green means you're within budget. Red means you've exceeded it. If you set the budget parameter to 0, set it to your actual monthly budget target.

tiles.title: Budget burn rate (this month)
tiles.description: The line shows your daily cumulative spend vs. the budget line. If you're above the line, you'll exceed budget. If you're below, you're on track. Act while there's still time.

tiles.title: Budget variance by subscription
tiles.description: Which subscriptions are over budget and by how much? These are the conversations to have this week, not at the end of the month when the bill is already set.

tiles.markdownText:
###  Chargeback & Showback
Break down costs by tag key for internal chargebacks, or view showback summaries by department/cost center. Use the **Tag Key** parameter to select which tag to group by. Common enterprise tags: `Department`, `CostCenter`, `BusinessUnit`, `Owner`.

tiles.title: Chargeback by tag value (last month)
tiles.description: Cost allocated by tag value for internal billing. This is what you send to teams to show them what they owe. Make sure the tag key used is the right one for your org.

tiles.title: Showback by department (last 3 months)
tiles.description: Department-level cost visibility over 3 months. Use this in meetings with department heads to show spending trends and set expectations for the next quarter.

tiles.markdownText:
###  Compute Architecture Analysis
CPU cost breakdown by architecture: **AMD** (a-series), **Intel** (standard series), and **Arm64/Cobalt** (p-series). GPU cost breakdown by N-series family: **NC** (training/inference), **ND** (distributed training), **NV** (visualization). Based on Azure VM SKU naming conventions. *(Community request [#1594](https://github.com/microsoft/finops-toolkit/issues/1594))*

tiles.title: Cost by CPU architecture (last month)
tiles.description: How much are you spending on ARM64 vs x86 compute? ARM instances are often cheaper. A large x86 slice might mean migration opportunities for cost savings.

tiles.markdownText:
### Warning: Data Quality Warnings
Flags cost rows where `EffectiveCost > ListCost`, which may indicate data quality issues from your billing provider (known MCA issue [#1424](https://github.com/microsoft/finops-toolkit/issues/1424)). Review affected services and subscriptions below.

tiles.title: Data quality warnings (last month)
tiles.description: Specific data quality issues found in last month's data. Fix these upstream if possible - bad data leads to bad decisions. Common issues: missing costs, negative amounts, cost mismatches.

tiles.markdownText:
# Multi-Cloud Distribution
Breakdown of costs across cloud providers. Use the **Provider Provider** filter to focus on a specific cloud. Data is grouped by the FOCUS ProviderName column.

tiles.title: Cost by provider
tiles.description: If you use multiple clouds, this shows where the money goes. A provider with a surprisingly large slice might warrant a closer look at rate optimization on that platform.

tiles.title: Monthly trend by provider
tiles.description: Is your multi-cloud spending shifting over time? Watch which provider's band is growing. If one is growing faster than expected, check if workloads migrated or if rates changed.

tiles.title: Top resources by provider (last n months)
tiles.description: Your most expensive resources across all cloud providers. In multi-cloud, this helps you spot where the real money is going regardless of which platform hosts it.

tiles.markdownText:
# Multi-Cloud Invoice Breakdown
Costs grouped by cloud provider and invoice issuer across billing periods. Uses the FOCUS InvoiceIssuerName column to identify the billing entity.

tiles.title: Cost by provider and invoice issuer
tiles.description: When reconciling invoices across cloud providers, this shows who billed what. Useful for multi-cloud orgs that need to match invoices to provider contracts.

tiles.markdownText:
# Provider Provider Comparison
Side-by-side comparison of costs, services, and savings rates across cloud providers. Use this page to understand spending distribution and identify provider-specific optimization opportunities.
> **Experimental** - This page shows multi-cloud data from the FOCUS ProviderName column. Data availability depends on which providers export cost data to your FinOps Hub.

tiles.title: Monthly cost by provider
tiles.description: Monthly spend by cloud provider. Watch for one provider growing disproportionately - it might mean workloads shifted or a pricing change took effect.

tiles.title: Daily cost by provider (last n days)
tiles.description: Recent daily spending by provider. Use this to catch sudden spikes on a specific cloud platform that might indicate a runaway deployment or misconfiguration.

tiles.title: Top services across providers
tiles.description: Compares equivalent services across clouds. If you're paying more for 'Compute' on one cloud than another, dig into why - it might be a right-sizing or pricing issue.

tiles.title: Sub accounts by provider
tiles.description: Your subscriptions/accounts by cloud, with cost and service details. Use this to ensure every account has an owner and to spot accounts with surprisingly high costs.

tiles.title: Savings rate by provider
tiles.description: How much are you saving on each cloud? The gap between list and effective cost shows your discount effectiveness. A low savings rate on a high-spend provider is a big opportunity.

**3 FinOps Maturity questions covered below:**

[What is our overall FinOps maturity score?](?tile=94ebe664-a687-4309-b0ce-d52a73fa85f0)  ·  [Which capabilities are at Crawl, Walk, or Run?](?tile=7bdc738f-24af-4732-8c6f-8ced6b068959)  ·  [What specific actions should we take next?](?tile=a76e5d07-b38d-4b1a-992a-f1ed1b7e1c0e)

---
### Unit FinOps Maturity Scorecard
Auto-scored assessment of your FinOps capabilities based on **live data signals**. Scores use the **Crawl -> Walk -> Run** maturity model from the [FinOps Framework](https://www.finops.org/framework/maturity-model/).

| Level | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| **Run** | Mature - automated, optimized, well-governed |
| **Walk** | Developing - processes in place, room to improve |
| **Crawl** | Starting - basic visibility, manual processes |

tiles.title: Overall Maturity Score
tiles.description: Where does your organization sit on the FinOps maturity scale? Crawl means you're just starting. Walk means you have processes. Run means you're optimizing proactively. What's your next step?

tiles.title: Capability Maturity Detail
tiles.description: Scores for each FinOps capability based on your actual data. Red areas are where you're weakest. Focus improvement efforts on capabilities that impact the most spend.

tiles.title: Improvement Recommendations
tiles.description: Concrete next steps to improve your maturity score. These are prioritized by impact - start at the top and work your way down. Each recommendation links to a specific capability gap.

### Project Budgets (Preview)
*Experimental - Track budgets for subscriptions, resource groups, or custom project groupings.*

This page lets you define project-level budgets and track actual spend against them. Unlike standard monthly budget tracking, Project Budgets supports:
- Subscription **Subscription budgets** - Track one or more subscriptions as a project
- RG **Resource group budgets** - Group resource groups into project units
- Unit **Custom groupings** - Combine scopes for cross-team visibility

**Setup:** Create a `ProjectBudgets` table in your ADX database:
`
.create table ProjectBudgets (ProjectName:string, ScopeType:string, ScopeFilter:string, MonthlyBudget:real)
`
Then add rows: `.ingest inline into table ProjectBudgets <| Platform Migration,Subscription,sub-prod-001,75000`

Below shows cost by subscription - use this as the basis for project budget tracking.

# Orphaned Resources (Preview) (Heuristic Detection)

Identify likely orphaned and idle resources using **cost-based heuristic patterns** from your FOCUS cost data.

## How It Works
| Detection Rule | Logic | Confidence |
|----------------|-------|------------|
| **Unattached Disk** | `Microsoft.Compute/disks` with cost but no VM compute in the same resource group | Medium |
| **Unused Public IP** | `Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses` with charges but no associated VM or LB in the subscription | Low |
| **Idle Load Balancer** | `Microsoft.Network/loadBalancers` with minimal cost (<$50/mo) | Low |

> Warning: **Heuristic detection may produce false positives.** Resources flagged as orphaned may still be in use for DR, staging, or compliance. This does not replace [Azure Advisor](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/advisor/) which has access to utilization metrics. See [#1602](https://github.com/microsoft/finops-toolkit/issues/1602) for the full Advisor integration roadmap.

CostsByMonth
| summarize BilledCost = round(sum(BilledCost), 2), EffectiveCost = round(sum(EffectiveCost), 2) by BillingPeriodStart = startofmonth(BillingPeriodStart)

CostsByMonth
// | summarize BilledCost = round(sum(BilledCost), 2), EffectiveCost = round(sum(EffectiveCost), 2) by BillingPeriodStart = startofmonth(BillingPeriodStart)
// | render timechart
| summarize BilledCost = sum(BilledCost), EffectiveCost = sum(EffectiveCost) by BillingPeriodStart = startofmonth(BillingPeriodStart)
| order by BillingPeriodStart asc
| extend PreviousBilledCost = prev(BilledCost)
| extend PreviousEffectiveCost = prev(EffectiveCost)
| project BillingPeriodStart
    , BilledCost = iif(isempty(PreviousBilledCost), todouble(0), todouble((BilledCost - PreviousBilledCost) * 100.0 / PreviousBilledCost))
    , EffectiveCost = iif(isempty(PreviousEffectiveCost), todouble(0), todouble((EffectiveCost - PreviousEffectiveCost) * 100.0 / PreviousEffectiveCost))

CostsPlus
| where startofmonth(ChargePeriodStart) >= startofmonth(now(), -1)
| where x_AmortizationClass != 'Principal'
// FX fix: reconstruct ContractedCost in billing currency from USD unit prices
| extend ContractedCostFX = ContractedUnitPrice * ConsumedQuantity * coalesce(x_BillingExchangeRate, real(1))
| summarize 
    EffectiveCost = sum(EffectiveCost),
    ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCostFX),
    ListCost = sum(ListCost)
    by
    ChargePeriodStart,
    Month = strcat(format_datetime(ChargePeriodStart, 'MM '), monthname[monthofyear(ChargePeriodStart)])
| extend CommitmentDiscountSavings = max_of(ContractedCost - EffectiveCost, real(0))
| extend NegotiatedDiscountSavings = max_of(ListCost - ContractedCost, real(0))
| order by ChargePeriodStart asc
| extend EffectiveCostRunningTotal = row_cumsum(EffectiveCost, prev(Month) != Month)
| extend CommitmentDiscountSavingsRunningTotal = row_cumsum(CommitmentDiscountSavings, prev(Month) != Month)
| extend NegotiatedDiscountSavingsRunningTotal = row_cumsum(NegotiatedDiscountSavings, prev(Month) != Month)
| project ChargePeriodStart, CommitmentDiscountSavingsRunningTotal, NegotiatedDiscountSavingsRunningTotal, EffectiveCostRunningTotal, Month
| render areachart

    // FX fix: reconstruct ContractedCost in billing currency from USD unit prices

    | summarize 
        BilledCost = sum(BilledCost),
        EffectiveCost = sum(EffectiveCost),
        ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCostFX),

let monthname = dynamic(['', 'January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June', 'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']);
let data = (
    CostsByMonth
    | where x_AmortizationClass != 'Principal'
    // FX fix: reconstruct ContractedCost in billing currency from USD unit prices
    | extend ContractedCostFX = ContractedUnitPrice * ConsumedQuantity * coalesce(x_BillingExchangeRate, real(1))
    | summarize 
        BilledCost     = todouble(round(sum(BilledCost), 2)),
        EffectiveCost  = todouble(round(sum(EffectiveCost), 2)),
        ContractedCostFX = todouble(round(sum(ContractedCostFX), 2)),
        ListCost       = todouble(round(sum(ListCost), 2)),
        CommitmentDiscountSavingsRaw = todouble(round(sum(todouble(ContractedCost) - todouble(EffectiveCost)), 2)),
        NegotiatedDiscountSavingsRaw = todouble(round(sum(todouble(ListCost) - todouble(ContractedCost)), 2)),
        TotalSavingsRaw = todouble(round(sum(todouble(ListCost) - todouble(EffectiveCost)), 2))
        by
        BillingPeriodStart
    | order by BillingPeriodStart asc
    | extend CommitmentDiscountSavingsFX = todouble(round(max_of(ContractedCostFX - EffectiveCost, real(0)), 2))
    | extend NegotiatedDiscountSavingsFX = todouble(round(max_of(ListCost - ContractedCostFX, real(0)), 2))
    | extend AttributedSavingsFX = todouble(round(NegotiatedDiscountSavingsFX + CommitmentDiscountSavingsFX, 2))
    | extend FXAdjustment = todouble(round(TotalSavingsRaw - AttributedSavingsFX, 2))
    | extend BillingPeriod = strcat(format_datetime(BillingPeriodStart, 'yyyy-MM - '), monthname[monthofyear(BillingPeriodStart)])
);

let monthname = dynamic(['', 'January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May', 'June', 'July', 'August', 'September', 'October', 'November', 'December']);
let data = (
    CostsByMonth
    | summarize 
        BilledCost     = sum(BilledCost),
        EffectiveCost  = sum(EffectiveCost),
        ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCost),
        ListCost       = sum(ListCost),
        SubAccountName = take_any(SubAccountName)
        by
        ChargePeriodStart = startofmonth(ChargePeriodStart),
        SubAccountId
    | as per
    | union (
        per
        | summarize 
            BilledCost     = sum(BilledCost),
            EffectiveCost  = sum(EffectiveCost),
            ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCost),
            ListCost       = sum(ListCost),
            SubAccountName = take_any(SubAccountName)
            by
            SubAccountId
    )
    | order by ChargePeriodStart asc
    | extend BilledCost                = todouble(round(BilledCost, 2))
    | extend EffectiveCost             = todouble(round(EffectiveCost, 2))
    | extend ContractedCost            = todouble(round(ContractedCost, 2))
    | extend ListCost                  = todouble(round(ListCost, 2))
    | extend ContractedCostNormalized  = min_of(ContractedCost, ListCost)
    | extend EffectiveCostNormalized   = min_of(EffectiveCost, ContractedCostNormalized)
    | extend CommitmentDiscountSavings = todouble(round(ContractedCostNormalized - EffectiveCostNormalized, 2))
    | extend NegotiatedDiscountSavings = todouble(round(ListCost - ContractedCostNormalized, 2))
    | extend ChargePeriod = iff(isempty(ChargePeriodStart), strcat('Total'), strcat(format_datetime(ChargePeriodStart, 'yyyy-MM - '), monthname[monthofyear(ChargePeriodStart)]))
);
data | evaluate pivot(ChargePeriod, sum(EffectiveCost), SubAccountName)
| order by column_ifexists('Total', 0.0) desc

        BilledCost     = sum(BilledCost),
        EffectiveCost  = sum(EffectiveCost),
        ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCost),
        ListCost       = sum(ListCost),
        SubAccountName = take_any(SubAccountName)
        by
        ChargePeriodStart,
        SubAccountId
    | as per
    | union (
        per
        | summarize 
            BilledCost     = sum(BilledCost),
            EffectiveCost  = sum(EffectiveCost),
            ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCost),
            ListCost       = sum(ListCost),
            SubAccountName = take_any(SubAccountName)
            by
            SubAccountId
    )
    | order by ChargePeriodStart asc
    | extend BilledCost                = todouble(round(BilledCost, 2))
    | extend EffectiveCost             = todouble(round(EffectiveCost, 2))
    | extend ContractedCost            = todouble(round(ContractedCost, 2))
    | extend ListCost                  = todouble(round(ListCost, 2))
    | extend ContractedCostNormalized  = min_of(ContractedCost, ListCost)
    | extend EffectiveCostNormalized   = min_of(EffectiveCost, ContractedCostNormalized)
    | extend CommitmentDiscountSavings = todouble(round(ContractedCostNormalized - EffectiveCostNormalized, 2))
    | extend NegotiatedDiscountSavings = todouble(round(ListCost - ContractedCostNormalized, 2))
    | extend ChargePeriod = iff(isempty(ChargePeriodStart), strcat('Total'), strcat(format_datetime(ChargePeriodStart, 'yyyy-MM - '), monthname[monthofyear(ChargePeriodStart)]))
);

| order by column_ifexists('Total', 0.0) desc

| union (costs | summarize Value = tostring(dcount(ResourceType)) by Label = "Resource types", Order = 11)
| union (costs | summarize Count = dcount(ResourceId) by Value = ResourceType | order by Count desc | limit 1 | extend Label = "Most used", Order = 21)
| union (costs | summarize sum(EffectiveCost) by Value = ResourceType | order by sum_EffectiveCost desc | limit 1 | extend Label = "Most cost", Order = 22)
| union (costs | summarize AllTypes = dcount(ResourceType), CommittedTypes = dcountif(ResourceType, isnotempty(CommitmentDiscountType)) by Label = "Covered by commitment discounts", Order = 31 | extend Value = strcat(round(1.0 * CommittedTypes / AllTypes * 100, 1), '%'))
| union (costs | summarize Savings = sum(ListCost - EffectiveCost) by Value = ResourceType | order by Savings desc | limit 1 | extend Label = "Most savings", Order = 32)
| union (costs | summarize CostPerResource = sum(EffectiveCost) / dcount(ResourceId) by Value = ResourceType | order by CostPerResource desc | limit 1 | extend Label = "Most expensive (cost / resource)", Order = 41)
| union (costs | where ResourceType !in (CostsPlus | where ChargePeriodStart < ago(numberOfDays * 1d) - 1d | distinct ResourceType) | summarize Count = dcount(ResourceId) by ResourceType | order by Count desc | as d | count | extend Label = "New in last n days", Value = case(Count == 0, '(none)', Count == 1, toscalar(d | project ResourceType), strcat(toscalar(d | limit 1 | project ResourceType), ' and ', (Count - 1), ' more')), Order = 42)

let data = materialize(
    CostsByMonth
    //
    // Don't double-count commitment discount purchases
    | where x_AmortizationClass != 'Principal'
    //
    | summarize 
        ListCost = sum(ListCost),
        ContractedCost = sum(ContractedCost),
        EffectiveCost = sum(EffectiveCost)
    | extend TotalSavings = ListCost - EffectiveCost
    | extend EffectiveSavingsRate = TotalSavings / ListCost

let MACCData = union isfuzzy=true
    (MACC_Lots | extend DataSource = 'API', SourcePriority = 2),
    (MACC_Lots_raw | extend DataSource = 'API', SourcePriority = 2),
    (MACC_ManualEntry | extend DataSource = 'Manual', SourcePriority = 1),
    (print Description='No MACC data configured', Currency='', StartDate=datetime(null), ExpirationDate=datetime(null), CommitmentAmount=0.0, DataSource='Fallback', SourcePriority=0)
| extend StartDate = todatetime(coalesce(column_ifexists('StartDate', datetime(null)), column_ifexists('PurchasedDate', datetime(null)), startofmonth(now())))
| extend ExpirationDate = todatetime(coalesce(column_ifexists('ExpirationDate', datetime(null)), endofmonth(now())))
| extend CommitmentAmount = todouble(coalesce(column_ifexists('CommitmentAmount', real(null)), column_ifexists('OriginalAmount', real(null))))
| extend IsActive = iff(isnotnull(ExpirationDate) and ExpirationDate >= now(), 1, 0)
| top 1 by SourcePriority desc, IsActive desc, StartDate desc;
let StartDate = toscalar(MACCData | project StartDate);
let EndDate = toscalar(MACCData | project ExpirationDate);
let Commitment = toscalar(MACCData | project CommitmentAmount);

| extend CurrencyCode = tostring(coalesce(column_ifexists('BillingCurrency', ''), column_ifexists('PricingCurrency', '')))

| summarize MonthlyConsumption = sum(EffectiveCost) by Month = startofmonth(ChargePeriodStart)
| order by Month asc
| extend RunningTotal = row_cumsum(MonthlyConsumption)
| extend CommitmentLine = Commitment
| extend RemainingBalance = Commitment - RunningTotal
| project Month, MonthlyConsumption = round(MonthlyConsumption, 0), RunningTotal = round(RunningTotal, 0), RemainingBalance = round(RemainingBalance, 0)

// 90-day cost forecast using time-series decomposition
let costs = Costs
| where ChargePeriodStart >= startofmonth(now(), -numberOfMonths) and ChargePeriodStart < startofday(now())
| extend CurrencyCode = tostring(coalesce(column_ifexists('BillingCurrency', ''), column_ifexists('PricingCurrency', '')))
| where isnull(currencyFilter) or tostring(currencyFilter) in ('', '*', 'All', 'all') or CurrencyCode == tostring(currencyFilter);
let startDate = toscalar(costs | summarize min(startofday(ChargePeriodStart)));
let endDate = toscalar(costs | summarize max(startofday(ChargePeriodStart)));
costs
| make-series DailyCost = sum(EffectiveCost) default=real(null) on ChargePeriodStart from startDate to endDate + 90d step 1d
| extend Forecast = series_decompose_forecast(DailyCost, 90)
| mv-expand ChargePeriodStart to typeof(datetime), DailyCost to typeof(real), Forecast to typeof(real)
| project Day = ChargePeriodStart,
    Actual = iff(ChargePeriodStart <= endDate and isnotnull(DailyCost), round(DailyCost, 2), real(null)),
    Forecast = iff(ChargePeriodStart > endDate and isnotnull(Forecast), round(max_of(0.0, Forecast), 2), real(null))

| summarize Actual = sum(EffectiveCost) by Month = startofmonth(ChargePeriodStart);
let recentAvg = toscalar(history | top maxGroupCount by Month desc | summarize avg(Actual));
history
| project Month, Actual = round(Actual, 0), Forecast = real(null)
| union (print Month = startofmonth(datetime_add('month', 1, now())), Actual = real(null), Forecast = round(recentAvg, 0))
| union (print Month = startofmonth(datetime_add('month', 2, now())), Actual = real(null), Forecast = round(recentAvg * 1.02, 0))
| union (print Month = startofmonth(datetime_add('month', 3, now())), Actual = real(null), Forecast = round(recentAvg * 1.04, 0))
| order by Month asc
| project Month, Actual, Forecast

Costs

| extend NormalizedSku = tolower(SkuRaw)
| where not(NormalizedSku matches regex @'(^|[^a-z0-9])n[cdv][0-9]')

    NormalizedSku matches regex @'(^|[^a-z0-9])[a-z]{1,3}[0-9]+p(s|d|v)?(_|[^a-z0-9]|$)', 'Arm64 (Cobalt/Ampere)',
    NormalizedSku matches regex @'(^|[^a-z0-9])[a-z]{1,3}[0-9]+a(s|d|v)?(_|[^a-z0-9]|$)', 'AMD',
    NormalizedSku matches regex @'(^|[^a-z0-9])[a-z]{1,3}[0-9]+(s|d|v)?(_|[^a-z0-9]|$)', 'Intel',
    'Unknown')
| summarize TotalCost = round(sum(EffectiveCost), 0), VMs = dcount(ResourceId) by Architecture
| order by TotalCost desc

let costs = CostsByMonth;
costs
| extend CurrencyCode = tostring(coalesce(column_ifexists('BillingCurrency', ''), column_ifexists('PricingCurrency', '')))
| where isnull(currencyFilter) or tostring(currencyFilter) in ('', '*', 'All', 'all') or CurrencyCode == tostring(currencyFilter)
| where isnotempty(RegionName) and RegionName !in ('', 'Unknown', 'Global', 'global')
| summarize
    TotalCost     = round(sum(EffectiveCost), 2),
    ResourceCount = dcountif(ResourceId, isnotempty(ResourceId))
    by RegionName
| join kind=inner AzureRegionGeo on RegionName
| project
    RegionName,
    Longitude,
    Latitude,
    TotalCost,
    ResourceCount
| order by ResourceCount desc, TotalCost desc

let periodStart = startofmonth(now(), -1);
let periodEnd = startofmonth(now());
let scoped =
    CostsByMonth
    | where BillingPeriodStart >= periodStart and BillingPeriodStart < periodEnd
    | where x_AmortizationClass != 'Principal'
    | project
        ListCost = todouble(ListCost),
        ContractedCost = todouble(ContractedCost),
        EffectiveCost = todouble(EffectiveCost),
        EffectiveCostAbs = abs(todouble(EffectiveCost));
let totals = scoped
| summarize
    Rows = count(),
    ListLtContracted = countif(ListCost < ContractedCost),
    ContractedLtEffective = countif(ContractedCost < EffectiveCost),
    InvertedRows = countif(ListCost < ContractedCost or ContractedCost < EffectiveCost),
    InvertedEffectiveCost = sumif(EffectiveCostAbs, ListCost < ContractedCost or ContractedCost < EffectiveCost),
    TotalEffectiveCost = sum(EffectiveCostAbs);
totals
| extend DataQualityStatus = iff(InvertedRows == 0, 'OK', 'Warning')
| extend InvertedRowPercent = iff(Rows > 0, round(100.0 * todouble(InvertedRows) / todouble(Rows), 2), 0.0)
| extend InvertedCostPercent = iff(TotalEffectiveCost > 0, round(100.0 * InvertedEffectiveCost / TotalEffectiveCost, 2), 0.0)
| project
    DataQualityStatus,
    Rows,
    InvertedRows,
    InvertedRowPercent,
    ListLtContracted,
    ContractedLtEffective,
    InvertedEffectiveCost = round(InvertedEffectiveCost, 2),
    TotalEffectiveCost = round(TotalEffectiveCost, 2),
    InvertedCostPercent

let lookbackMonths = 6;
let analysisStart = startofmonth(now(), -lookbackMonths);
CostsByMonth
| where BillingPeriodStart >= analysisStart
| where ChargeCategory == 'Purchase'
| summarize TotalCommitmentCharge = round(sum(BilledCost), 2), MonthsWithCharges = dcount(startofmonth(BillingPeriodStart)) by CommitmentType = coalesce(CommitmentDiscountType, 'Other purchases')
| extend AvgMonthlyCommitmentBurn = round(TotalCommitmentCharge / toreal(lookbackMonths), 2)
| order by AvgMonthlyCommitmentBurn desc

let lookbackMonths = 6;
let analysisStart = startofmonth(now(), -lookbackMonths);
CostsByMonth
| where BillingPeriodStart >= analysisStart
| where ChargeCategory == 'Purchase'
| summarize CommitmentBurn = round(sum(BilledCost), 2) by BillingPeriodStart = startofmonth(BillingPeriodStart), CommitmentType = coalesce(CommitmentDiscountType, 'Other purchases')
| order by BillingPeriodStart asc
